meta.title: Causes of World War I
meta.subject: World War I causation — a July crisis inside a preloaded European system

scenes.title: Causes of World War I
scenes.tagline: A murder in Sarajevo mattered because Europe was already primed for escalation
scenes.footnote: Wikipedia source page   ·   reviewed as an argument, not a timeline   ·   surveyed past the one-bullet myth

beats.narration: This is not a film about every grievance before 1914. It is about one harder claim: why a local assassination could widen into a general European war.
The misconception to kill is the schoolbook version that one bullet caused World War I. The article's real argument is rougher: the bullet was the trigger, but Europe was already loaded.

So we are not relaying the page section by section. We are testing its core explanation: long-term polarization, short-term July decisions, and the trade-off that comes with calling the outbreak overdetermined.

scenes.heading: The page proves late July best

nodes.label: Competing alignments
nodes.sub: real camps, but not automatic dominoes

nodes.label: Arms and timetables
nodes.sub: rail mobilization, war plans, offensive doctrine

beats.narration: The page's strongest-earned claim is not every deep cause it names. It is the late-July step from Balkan crisis to continental war. Rival alignments, military preparation, and imperial fear stay on the board as pressure, but not with the same proof.
The assassination mattered because Vienna read it as a casus belli and an existential challenge to a multi-national empire, not because one murder mechanically forces a continent into battle.

The alignments mattered too, but with one corrective the page is careful about: Britain, France, and Russia were not a perfectly automatic alliance machine. The camps were real, yet this is background pressure shaping choices, not proof of a treaty reflex.

And beneath the diplomacy sat the harder machinery: rail mobilization and offensive war plans already in place. The page uses that background best not to rank a deep cause, but to explain why, once July decisions were taken, diplomacy lost slack fast.

scenes.heading: Long-term pressure met one short month

beats.narration: The article runs on two clocks at once. One is the long clock: decades of alignment. The other is the short clock: a single month between Sarajevo and war.

By 1914 the long clock had already passed through Moroccan, Bosnian, and Balkan crises. The short clock added Berlin's blank cheque and Vienna's ultimatum, which made the local dispute harder to contain.
Then the two clocks touched. Arms races and war plans were ready before the shooting started; once mobilization began, diplomacy had less room to stall or reverse the slide.
That is the page's real explanation. Not inevitability in 1887, and not accident in Sarajevo. Convergence in late July 1914.

scenes.heading: The usable counts are structural, not calendar math

metrics.label: Causes the thesis says can suffice

beats.narration: The usable numbers in the notes are structural, not calendar arithmetic. Start with two bad models the page has to kill: one bullet as the whole cause, and alliances as automatic dominoes.
Then two causal layers. The notes split long-term structural tension from short-term July decisions, which is why Sarajevo matters as a trigger without pretending it explains the whole war by itself.

A third two is operational. German planning assumes a two-front war against France and Russia, so once Russia moves, speed in the west stops being background militarism and becomes direct pressure inside the crisis.

And the page's own thesis lands on the tightest count of all: one or two causes could have launched the war. That is why overdetermination is earned for escalation. It says the crisis had more than one sufficient wire, not that the deeper catalog has been cleanly ranked.

scenes.heading: The article rejects single-cause history
scenes.text: Indeed, the search for a single main cause is not a helpful approach to history. Instead, there are multiple causes any one or two of which could have launched the war. The fact that so many plausible explanations for the outbreak of the war have been advanced over the years indicates on the one hand that it was massively overdetermined, and on the other that no effort to analyze the causal factors involved can ever fully succeed.

marks.quote: not a helpful approach to history
marks.note: the page explicitly rejects the one-cause frame

marks.quote: multiple causes any one or two of which could have launched the war

marks.note: more than one path ran from crisis to catastrophe

beats.narration: Here is the source's ruling on method, but it has to be earned. The notes earn it in a specific July chain: Vienna treats Sarajevo as a casus belli, Berlin gives the blank cheque, France reaffirms Russia, Russia's partial mobilization collapses into general mobilization, and German planning assumes a two-front war.

That is what makes the phrase multiple causes persuasive here. This chain shows more than one sufficient wire inside late July, so overdetermination is earned as an escalation account. What it does not do is settle the deeper ranking question about which long-run cause mattered most.

Where the page is weaker is the deeper catalog. Imperial rivalry, Social Darwinism, and other long-run pressures are named more than decisively ranked, and some nearby claims even carry citation-needed flags. So overdetermined is earned strongest as an escalation account, not as a precise ordering of every deep cause.
Start at the trigger. Franz Ferdinand is assassinated in Sarajevo on 28 June, and Vienna reads the killing not as one outrage among many but as a direct challenge to imperial survival.
Austria-Hungary does not move in isolation. Before striking Serbia, Vienna asks Berlin for backing against the wider consequences.
Berlin answers with the blank cheque. Support first, localization second. That is the point where a punitive Balkan war becomes much harder to keep Balkan.
Vienna then issues the harsh ultimatum and moves against Serbia, knowing Russia is Serbia's major supporter. The crisis is now testing the wider system, not just punishing one state.

Russia first tries partial mobilization, then discovers its own plans do not bend cleanly enough to stop there. By 30 July it has moved to general mobilization, along both the Austrian and German fronts.

By then France has already reaffirmed Russia, so German planners now see the two-front war they feared as live, not hypothetical. Their answer is a rapid western attack before Russia fully ripens. The local crisis did not vanish. It propagated through prior commitments and war plans.

Here is the hard boundary. The Triple Entente was not an automatic mutual-defense alliance, and Britain still retained policy freedom in 1914. So the article breaks if you read alignment as compulsion. It still holds on the narrower claim the notes support: alignments explain pressure and shrinking options, not an automatic British march to war.

That does not make the widening mechanically inevitable. It does show why late July breaks faster than diplomacy can repair it. Choice remains in principle, but it is now operating inside a collapsing crisis window.

scenes.heading: Overdetermination explains the mechanism, at the cost of clean blame

nodes.label: Systemic account
nodes.sub: many live causes, crowded into one crisis

nodes.label: Risk — agency blurs
nodes.sub: many causes can wash out late-July choices

nodes.label: Why choose it
nodes.sub: it explains how a Balkan murder widened

nodes.label: What keeps blame sharp
nodes.sub: the page still gives Germany a larger share

beats.narration: Here is the trade-off. This account chooses the systemic view over the satisfying one-culprit story because the system view explains the mechanism of escalation. It shows why one assassination could widen, instead of merely naming who to hate.
But that choice pays a real bill. If every wire matters, agency can blur into atmosphere. Overdetermination can fail into a shrug: everyone caused it, therefore no one decisive did.

The page partly repairs that by keeping one sharper claim in reserve: later scholarly consensus still gives Germany a larger share of responsibility. So the honest position is not single culprit instead of system, or system instead of blame. It is this: choose the systemic account to explain the outbreak, and keep the Germany caveat so nuance does not become evasion.

scenes.heading: Causes of World War I: an honest verdict
points: Disposition: persuasive on the late-July widening from Balkan crisis to European war.
Strongest proof: casus belli, blank cheque, France-Russia reaffirmation, mobilization chain, and two-front planning.
Weakest proof: deep causes are cataloged more than decisively ranked; some nearby claims carry citation-needed flags.
Residual risk: overdetermination can wash agency into atmosphere.
Watch this caveat: the page stays honest only if Germany's larger share of responsibility remains in view.

beats.narration: The ruling is mixed but clear. As an explanation of why late July widened a Balkan crisis into European war, the page is persuasive.

Its strongest evidence is specific: Vienna treats Sarajevo as a casus belli, Berlin issues the blank cheque, France reaffirms Russia, Russian mobilization broadens, and German two-front planning turns speed into pressure.
Its weakest stretch is deeper causation. Imperialism, ideology, and long-run rivalry are named, but the page does not decisively rank them, and some nearby claims even carry citation-needed flags.

The residual risk follows from that strength. Overdetermination can explain the widening and still blur agency into blame-washing if every background pressure is treated as equal to late-July decisions.

So the thing to watch is the caveat the page itself keeps: Germany still bears a larger share of responsibility. Keep that in frame, and the article reads as a strong escalation account rather than a soft excuse for everyone.
